From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Racing engine

The **Porsche 547 engine**, often called **Fuhrmann engine** after [Ernst Fuhrmann](/wiki/Ernst_Fuhrmann "Ernst Fuhrmann"), was introduced in 1954 by [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") to upgrade the mid-rear-engine [Porsche 550](/wiki/Porsche_550 "Porsche 550") sportscar. Still based on the Volkswagen design, thus an air-cooled [naturally-aspirated](/wiki/Naturally-aspirated "Naturally-aspirated") [flat-four](/wiki/Flat-four_engine "Flat-four engine") [boxer](/wiki/Boxer_engine "Boxer engine") [engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), it was intended for [racing](/wiki/Motor_racing "Motor racing") and fitted with no less than four camshafts (DOHC).

Fitted with the new engine that initially developed 110 hp from 1500cc, the Porsche 550 soon received a new frame and was called 550A. The 4-cam engine was also used in the successor [Porsche 718](/wiki/Porsche_718 "Porsche 718") that was even turned into Formula 2 and 1 cars. After the [1962 Formula One season](/wiki/1962_Formula_One_season "1962 Formula One season"), Porsche left F1 to re-focus on road-going sportscars, offering the *[Porsche 904](/wiki/Porsche_904 "Porsche 904") Carrera GTS* with the final edition of the Fuhrmann engine, some 180 hp from 2000cc.

By the time, there were also the flat-8 Type 771 [Porsche flat-eight engines](/wiki/Porsche_flat-eight_engines "Porsche flat-eight engines") and the new [Porsche flat-six engine](/wiki/Porsche_flat-six_engine "Porsche flat-six engine") Type 901 as introduced in the [Porsche 911 (classic)](/wiki/Porsche_911_(classic) "Porsche 911 (classic)"). The F6 design was chosen for the 1966 series of *[Porsche 906](/wiki/Porsche_906 "Porsche 906") Carrera 6*, making the F4 obsolete.

## Formula Racing History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_547_engine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Formula Racing History")]

The *Porsche 547/3* was used by [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") for [Formula One racing](/wiki/Formula_One_racing "Formula One racing"); between [1960](/wiki/1960_Formula_One_season "1960 Formula One season") and [1961](/wiki/1961_Formula_One_season "1961 Formula One season")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In October 1958, the [Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l%27Automobile "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile") (FIA) announced that for the [1961 Formula One season](/wiki/1961_Formula_One_season "1961 Formula One season"), engine capacity would be limited to the same 1.5 litres as in [Formula Two](/wiki/Formula_Two "Formula Two") (F2). This meant that Porsche could use their [Porsche 718](/wiki/Porsche_718 "Porsche 718")-based central steering and open wheel F2 cars almost unchanged in future F1. The [Porsche 787](/wiki/Porsche_787 "Porsche 787") would not get the eight-cylinder though, continuing with the air-cooled, [DOHC](/wiki/Overhead_camshaft#Dual_overhead_camshaft "Overhead camshaft") four-cylinder Type 547 [boxer engine](/wiki/Flat_engine#Boxer_engine "Flat engine") that had been developed by [Ernst Fuhrmann](/wiki/Ernst_Fuhrmann "Ernst Fuhrmann") and that had powered the 550 Spyders and [Porsche 718](/wiki/Porsche_718 "Porsche 718") series until then. It was powered by a 547/3 four-cylinder engine with Kugelfischer [fuel injection](/wiki/Fuel_injection "Fuel injection"). At Monaco the car retired when the fuel injection cut out. A second car, also fitted with the 547/3 engine, was completed in time to appear in the [1961 Dutch Grand Prix](/wiki/1961_Dutch_Grand_Prix "1961 Dutch Grand Prix") on 22 May alongside the other 787.

## Technical data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_547_engine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Technical data")]

| Porsche 787: | Detail[[3]](#cite_note-3) |
| --- | --- |
| Engine: | [Flat four-cylinder](/wiki/Flat-four_engine "Flat-four engine") [four-stroke](/wiki/Four-stroke_engine "Four-stroke engine") [boxer](/wiki/Flat_engine#Boxer_engine "Flat engine") Type 547/3 |
| Displacement: | 1,498 cc (91.4 cu in) |
| Bore × Stroke: | 85.0 mm × 66.0 mm (3.3 in × 2.6 in) |
| Maximum power: | 139 kW (189.0 PS; 186.4 hp) at 8000 rpm |
| Maximum torque: | 147 N⋅m (108.4 ft⋅lb) at 6500 rpm |
| Compression ratio: | 9.5:1-10.3:1[[4]](#cite_note-4) |
| Valvetrain: | One bevel-gear driveshaft and two overhead camshafts per cylinder bank (four in total). Two valves per cylinder. | |
| Cooling: | [Air-cooled](/wiki/Air-cooled_engine "Air-cooled engine") (fan) | |
